summary:
The War of the Austrian Succession (1740–1748 AD) was a major European conflict triggered by the death of Holy Roman Emperor Charles VI in 1740 without a male heir, leaving his daughter Maria Theresa to claim the Habsburg dominions despite widespread European challenges to female succession. [[event:2386|Frederick the Great]] of Prussia immediately seized the opportunity, invading and annexing the wealthy Austrian province of Silesia — triggering a war in which France, Bavaria, Spain, and Saxony allied against Austria, Britain, and the Dutch Republic. The war was fought across Europe, in the Americas ([[event:2456|King George's War]]), and in India, making it a precursor to the more globally decisive [[event:2332|Seven Years' War]].

Maria Theresa proved an unexpectedly formidable ruler: she rallied Hungarian support with an emotional appeal to the Hungarian Diet (appearing with her infant son in her arms), reorganized Austrian administration and military, and ultimately preserved the core Habsburg inheritance despite losing Silesia permanently. The war ended with the [[event:3278|Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle]] (1748), which largely restored the pre-war territorial situation — except for Silesia, which Prussia retained. The war's key outcome was the transformation of European alliance structures: the 'diplomatic revolution' of 1756 saw Austria ally with France (traditional enemies) and Britain ally with Prussia in a complete reversal of the previous alignment, setting up the Seven Years' War. Frederick the Great's military performance in the war established his reputation and Prussia's as a major European military power, beginning the long rivalry between Prussia and Austria for German leadership.

read_more_links.title: Frederick Great Silesia invasion Austrian succession
